parents.mother_research_notes: Abigail Goodrich (T01018's second wife, daughter of John and Abigail Deming Goodrich).

notable.notable_blurb: Third Chief of U.S. Army Ordnance, 1848-1851

military_service.war: War of 1812 + Mexican-American War + post-1812 Ordnance Corps
military_service.notes:
Career arc:
- 1813: Entered Army from NYC as 3rd Lt of Infantry; quickly promoted 2nd Lt; Aug 1813 transferred to Ordnance duty + 1st Lt.
- Initially commanded New York City Arsenal, then Albany Arsenal.
- May 1814: reverted to 1st Lt; retained on Ordnance duty post-War of 1812.
- Massachusetts: oversaw construction of new arsenal at Watertown.
- Allegheny Arsenal command (near Pittsburgh PA).
- 1821: transferred to 2nd Artillery, detailed to ordnance.
- Aug 1823: Brevet Major (10 years faithful service in one grade).
- Feb 1824 controversy: while at Allegheny Arsenal, ordered horsewhipping of escaped/recaptured deserter; court-martialed twice, both panels found him not guilty.
- Sept 1824 - Jan 1832: commanded Watervliet Arsenal NY.
- May 1832: promoted Lt Col; Inspector of Arsenals and Armories for the Ordnance Corps.
- April 1839: Acting Chief of Ordnance Bureau (under Van Buren administration).
- 1842: appointed Asst Chief of Ordnance (de facto running daily operations for 3 years).
- Mexican-American War: directed expansion of Ordnance Department responsibilities, new ordnance depots, field service operations.
- March 25, 1848: promoted Colonel, became 3rd Chief of Ordnance (after death of Col. George Bomford).
- March 3, 1849: Brevet Brigadier General (rank from May 30, 1848) for Mexican War service.
- 1849: Major Alfred Mordecai's elaborate study of European ordnance completed under Talcott's supervision; resulted in first comprehensive American field artillery system.
- Summer 1851: court-martialed at direction of Sec. of War Charles Conrad following dispute over munitions contracts to a Southern contractor — Bvt Col. Benjamin Huger had proceeded with the contract without Talcott's full knowledge or approval. Despite long-standing departmental policy authorizing Talcott to make open purchases at his discretion, he was dismissed from the Army. Huger was never charged but admonished by President Fillmore. Senate efforts to investigate or provide financial relief: failed.

narrative_blocks.header: Third Chief of Ordnance of the U.S. Army
narrative_blocks.body: George Talcott rose through the Ordnance Corps from 1813 lieutenant to 1848 Chief of Ordnance, supervising the major Ordnance Department expansion before and during the Mexican-American War. He received Brevet Brigadier General in 1849 for his Mexican War service and oversaw the 1849 Mordecai report that established the first comprehensive American field artillery system.

narrative_blocks.header: Court-martialed and dismissed in 1851 over munitions contracts
narrative_blocks.body: In summer 1851, Secretary of War Charles Conrad court-martialed Talcott over munitions contract awards to a Southern contractor — though Bvt. Col. Benjamin Huger had proceeded with the contract without Talcott's full knowledge or approval. Many felt the verdict unwarranted; Senate efforts to investigate or provide financial relief failed.

children.n: Bvt. Lt. Col. George Henry Talcott

parents.mother_research_notes: Abigail Goodrich (T01018's second wife, daughter of John and Abigail Deming Goodrich).

parents.father_research_notes: Isaac Henry Bogart (b. Sept 7, 1765, m. Cathlina Visscher Jan 31, 1789, d. Sept 22, 1841). Bogart family emigrated from Schoonderwoert, Holland to New Netherland (Beverwyck/Albany) ~1640: ancestor Theunis Bogart in Holland → son Cornelis → emigrant sons Guysbert and Cornelis (Cornelis lived/died Albany 1665; Guysbert removed to Catskill). 6 generations to Angelica.
parents.mother_research_notes: Cathlina Visscher (d. April 10, 1845). Visscher families also Dutch colonial Albany.

death.cause: Wounds received at Battle of Molino del Rey (Sept 8, 1847)

notable.notable_blurb: Mexican War: wounded at Molino del Rey 1847

education.institution: United States Military Academy at West Point

narrative_blocks.header: Wounded at Molino del Rey, never recovered
narrative_blocks.body: Major George Henry Talcott was wounded at the Battle of Molino del Rey on September 8, 1847 — the same day he was breveted Lieutenant Colonel for gallant and meritorious conduct. He never recovered, dying six and a half years later at age 42 at Indian Springs, Georgia. His widow Catharine was buried beside him in 1869 at Albany Rural Cemetery.

narrative_blocks.header: Six and a half years dying
narrative_blocks.body: The wound he took at Molino del Rey in September 1847 never healed; he died at Indian Springs, Georgia, six and a half years later — aged 42 years, 10 months, and 23 days, by the family’s exact counting.

bio.title: Bvt. Lt. Col.
bio.display_name: Bvt. Lt. Col. George Henry Talcott

parents.father_research_notes: Gen. W.W. Starke of Augusta, Georgia.

burial.plot_notes: Albany Rural Cemetery, Albany NY. Findagrave Memorial 11610472. GPS: 42.7058944, -73.7304611.

residence.notes: Residence at Albany NY for 40+ years.

notable.notable_blurb: Compiler of the 1876 Talcott Genealogy book

education.notes: Entered 1829, left in sophomore year for civil engineering career — one of the few Talcott book entries to attend Yale before leaving.

career.role: Civil Engineer — Hudson River Improvement at Albany
Civil Engineer — Erie Railroad Surveys (Dunkirk terminus)
career.notes: Worked alongside his uncle Andrew Talcott (T01807) who served as Adjunct Chief Engineer of NY&Erie Western Division 1836-37.

career.role: Civil Engineer — Mississippi River Mouth Improvement
Brigadier General + Quartermaster General of New York
career.organization: New York State Militia (under Gov. Horatio Seymour)
career.notes: Appointed by Gov. Seymour upon his election in 1862. Served through Seymour's administration with rank of Brigadier General.

career.organization: Self / Martin & Allardyce (publisher of Goodrich book)
career.notes: Authored 'Genealogical Notes of New York and New England Families' and 'Talcott Pedigree in England and America from 1558 to 1876' (the latter is the foundational Talcott genealogy used throughout this project as 'the Talcott book'). Also compiled 'The Goodrich family,' rearranged and published by Martin & Allardyce.

narrative_blocks.header: Brigadier General who compiled the Talcott Genealogy book
narrative_blocks.body: Sebastian Visscher Talcott left Yale in his sophomore year for civil engineering — surveying the US-Canada boundary, the Mississippi delta, and Lake Superior mineral lands. Appointed Quartermaster General of New York with rank of Brigadier General by Governor Horatio Seymour in 1862. He compiled the family's foundational genealogical works including the 1876 'Talcott Pedigree in England and America' that this project uses as its primary Talcott source.

narrative_blocks.header: Surveyed US-Canada boundary as civil engineer
narrative_blocks.body: Before his Civil War service he worked as a civil engineer across major mid-century projects: the United States–Canada boundary survey, the Hudson River improvement at Albany, the Erie Railroad primary surveys at the Dunkirk terminus, and the Mississippi mouth improvement under Professor Hassler. He also worked as assistant superintendent on the Lake Superior mineral lands.

narrative_blocks.header: New York Quartermaster General appointed by Seymour, 1862
narrative_blocks.body: In 1862 Governor Horatio Seymour appointed him Quartermaster General of New York with rank of Brigadier General. He served through Seymour's administration — handling supply, transport, and equipment for New York's massive Union Army contributions to the Civil War.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Olivia died ten months before he did
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Olivia Maria Shearman Talcott died at Albany on 29 January 1888 after forty-five years of marriage. He followed her ten months later on 10 November 1888 at age seventy-five; both buried at Albany Rural Cemetery.

cross_connections.display_label: Hooker compiled the parallel 1909 Hooker Genealogy book
cross_connections.description: Sebastian Visscher Talcott (1812-1888) compiled the foundational 'Talcott Pedigree in England and America, 1558-1876' (1876). Cmdr. Edward Hooker U.S.N. (1822-1903) compiled the parallel 'Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker, Hartford Connecticut, 1586-1908' (1909). The two men were distant cousins through the founding generation of Hartford, CT (Rev. Thomas Hooker and John Talcott both arrived 1636). These two volumes are the foundational primary sources for this entire genealogy project.

cross_connections.display_label: Compiled 1876 Talcott parallel to Whittlesey Memorials co-author
cross_connections.notes: Part of the writing-ancestries CC web — kindred family historians who compiled the foundational printed genealogies underlying this project: Henry Martyn Whittlesey's 1855 first Whittlesey family history (HD3790); Charles Barnes Whittlesey's co-compiled Whittlesey Memorials (X02790); Sebastian Visscher Talcott's Talcott Pedigree 1876 (T01819); Cmdr. Edward Hooker's Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker 1909 (H01628); and Sam Hooker's current digital genealogy project (HD3386).

cross_connections.display_label: Compiled 1876 Talcott parallel to Whittlesey's 1855 history
cross_connections.notes: Part of the writing-ancestries CC web — kindred family historians who compiled the foundational printed genealogies underlying this project: Henry Martyn Whittlesey's 1855 first Whittlesey family history (HD3790); Charles Barnes Whittlesey's co-compiled Whittlesey Memorials (X02790); Sebastian Visscher Talcott's Talcott Pedigree 1876 (T01819); Cmdr. Edward Hooker's Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker 1909 (H01628); and Sam Hooker's current digital genealogy project (HD3386).

cross_connections.display_label: Wrote 1874 Dwight and 1871 Strong family genealogies
the congressman he recorded in his genealogical notes

parents.father_research_notes: Robert Shearman (b. Sept 10, 1790; d. Sept 6, 1838). Descended from Philip Sherman (b. 1610 Dedham Essex Co England, m. Sarah Odding, d. 1686 Portsmouth RI — 13 children, Town Clerk Portsmouth, large landholder).
parents.mother_research_notes: Anna Maria Sherman (m. Robert Shearman Jan 8, 1822). Both Robert and Anna descended from Thomas Sherman of Yaxley England (d. 1564) — 5 generations to common Sherman ancestor; Robert spelt name 'Shearman,' Anna spelt 'Sherman.'

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Bradford of the Mayflower
narrative_blocks.body: George Talcott's first wife, Vienna Bradford, was a direct descendant of Governor William Bradford of the Mayflower. She died in 1785 at age 27. George remarried the following year, had six more children, and lived on the family homestead in Glastonbury — land his family had farmed since his grandfather's 1699 deed.

narrative_blocks.header: At the Long Island retreat with his father
narrative_blocks.body: He served in the Revolution alongside his father Col. Elizur and his brothers, present at the army’s night escape from Long Island. A Glastonbury farmer, he held the homestead that came down through his father.

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife traced to the Mayflower
narrative_blocks.body: He married Vienna Bradford in 1777 — of the Plymouth Bradford line — and lost her at twenty-seven. His second marriage, to Abigail Goodrich, lasted until his death from “inflammation of the brain” in 1813.

cross_connections.display_label: Hooker descendant served in his Troop of horses, 1757 alarm.

narrative_blocks.header: Chaired Glastonbury's denunciation of the Boston Port Bill
narrative_blocks.body: As chairman of the Glastonbury town meeting he led the formal denunciation of the Boston Port Bill — one of the acts that precipitated the Revolution. He already held a commission as Colonel of a Troop of Horse and would lead that troop into the field.

narrative_blocks.header: Carried home from Long Island on a litter
narrative_blocks.body: Present at the Battle of Long Island in August 1776 at age 67, he fell so ill during the campaign that he was carried back to Glastonbury on a litter. He resigned his commission three months later and never rejoined the Continental Army.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost a Susquehanna land claim in court
narrative_blocks.body: He held substantial Connecticut Susquehanna lands in Pennsylvania — claimed under Connecticut's sea-to-sea royal charter. He lost that investment entirely when the courts ruled against the Connecticut claim.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Ruth brought a large fortune to him
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Ruth Wright brought a substantial fortune to the marriage, inherited from her wealthy father Daniel Wright Jr. They married on his 21st birthday — December 31, 1730 — and son George later served with him in the Revolution.

narrative_blocks.header: He lost four sons to black canker
narrative_blocks.body: Of Colonel Elizur Talcott's twelve children with Ruth Wright, four young sons died of what the family record called 'black canker' between 1748 and 1750. He twice gave a dead son's name to a later child, and only a handful of the twelve lived to marry.

military_service.unit: Sixth Connecticut Militia (Sixth Regiment, Colony of Connecticut)
military_service.notes: Colonel of the Sixth Connecticut Militia 1775-1776. At age 67, present at the Battle of Long Island (Aug 27, 1776). Became ill during the campaign and was carried back to Glastonbury CT on a litter; resigned his commission three months later. Surviving documentation: a holograph order signed by Col. Elizur Talcott in Glastonbury CT June 12, 1776 to Capt. Benjamin Wright of the Fourth Company directing election of a new Lieutenant — the document was signed off by Titus Hosmer for the Lower House (Continental Congress delegate and Articles of Confederation signer) and George Wyllys for the Upper House (Connecticut Secretary of State). See DOC013.

Commanded a Troop of horses raised at Glastonbury for service in the alarm for the relief of Fort William Henry and parts adjacent, August 1757. His unit included Hooker descendant Samuel Hooker Jr (H00228) for 14 days of service. Source: Berlin-Peck Memorial Library — Berlin in the French and Indian War 1753-1763.

burial.plot_notes: Quarryville Cemetery, Bolton CT. FindAGrave Memorial ID 10706544.

narrative_blocks.header: She brought a fortune to her marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Ruth Wright was an only child who inherited a large fortune from her wealthy father and brought it to her marriage with Elizur Talcott. Family memory described her as a remarkably handsome woman, tall and erect, with golden hair and black eyes.

INST035.name: United States Military Academy (West Point)
INST035.notes: Founded 1802 at West Point NY. HOOKER TREE CONNECTION: Rev. George Washington Woodbridge (HD2163), son of General Jonathan Woodbridge (H00928), graduated West Point; served as 2nd Lieutenant US Army 1826-1829 before entering the Episcopal ministry. He became rector of Monumental Church Richmond VA 1843-1878 and escorted Flora Cooke Stuart to her dying husband Jeb Stuart in May 1864.
